Kerala.introduction: While Kerala is often portrayed as a monolithic cultural entity, the reality is a mosaic of tribal communities, ancient craft guilds, and linguistic minorities whose histories challenge the dominant narrative. Discover Kerala's unseen depths—beyond the backwaters.

items.title: Moosharis of Kunhimangalam
items.description: A heritage village home to 32 families who create intricate lamps and deities using the ancient Panchaloha (five-metal alloy) technique. This art form provides articles for Hindu temples, Christian churches, and Muslim mosques.

items.title: The Cholanaikkans
items.description: Hidden in the Nilambur forest, this isolated hunter-gatherer tribe numbers only a few hundred. They measure time by the sun and possess deep oral knowledge regarding medicinal plants and forest ecology.

items.title: Vadakke Madham Manuscripts
items.description: Preserved under strict climate control, this collection of 748 palm-leaf manuscripts (taliyola) encapsulates the intellectual history of the region, including Vedic philosophy, ritual, and literature.

items.title: Kanakkatikaram Mathematical Treatises
items.description: Elementary mathematical treatises focusing on measurements and practical calculations in pre-modern Kerala. They demonstrate how mathematical knowledge was transmitted in the vernacular Malayalam.

items.title: Mavilan Tulu Language
items.description: Mavilan Tulu is a distinct threatened language spoken by the Mavilan community, a Scheduled Tribe in the Kasargod district, currently holding a population of ~45,000 speakers with disrupted intergenerational transmission.

items.title: Arabi-Malayalam Script
items.description: A significant scriptorial variant of Malayalam using the Arabic script, popular among the Mappila Muslim community. Long marginalized and 'folklorized', it is now being analyzed as a distinct literary historiography.
